body: why are we asking for DRM formats?
author: pukkandan

body:
That's a good question that even I don't know the answer to. All I can say is that that was the post request sent to the API in browser, and that none of the formats in the m3u8 that are eventually returned are DRMed. Perhaps the nature of this request is a relic from when Fox did use DRM or it is in preparation for some future date when they do implement DRM. If you like, I can try to snoop the requests in a non-DRM browser (e.g. Firefox) and see if the request changes. But I also wouldn't put too much stock into the data sent here anyway, for example max resolution is listed as 720p, but if a video has 1080p formats available, they will still be returned. It seems the only things that really make a difference (aside from the structure of the data) are the device ID, which must match the ID used in the "login" request at initialization, and the stream ID, which must match the video ID.

EDIT: Just did open up a Firefox private browsing window with DRM disabled and the request was still the same.

```suggestion
    # Do not use re.verbose since value may contain unescaped spaces

        % {'attribute': re.escape(attribute), 'value': value, 'vqo': value_quote_optional})
```
It is simpler than your regex, passes all tests and should not cause any catastrophic backtracking as far as I can tell (since the `|` groups are mutually exclusive and there is no non-greedy matching)

body: Are you sure passing cookies don't work?
author: pukkandan

body: We must pass a value for `drmSupport` else the server will return a 403. The default value for the request sent by the browser is `DASH_WIDEVINE`, but unsurprisingly this returns a dash stream with Widevine DRM. If the value is set to just `HLS` instead, then we get an HLS stream with no DRM.
author: ischmidt20

body: Well passing cookies could work in theory, but it would require a completely different authentication process than what is used for the other streams. For example there is no equivalent or direct replacement for the `adobeToken` value that gets passed to the asset API call which returns the playlist URL. For ESPN+ streams to get the URL you need to make a call to `https://playback.svcs.plus.espn.com/media/***/scenarios/browser~ssai` with an authorization header, and I'm not sure how to get that header but after some research last night it looks like it could take a quite lengthy sequence of other API calls. Then you also need to get the authorization header to access the decryption key for the actual video. So ESPN+ is probably not impossible but would require a lot more time to figure out

```
Always move trailing parentheses after the last argument.
```

You dont have to change this, but FYI:

`dict_get(P, (a, b))` => `traverse_obj(P, a, b)`
`try_get(P, (lambda x: x[a][b], lambda x: x[c][d]), t)` => `traverse_obj(P, (a, b), (c, d), expected_type=t)`

body:
`.geturl()` is the right way to get redirect URL

HeadRequest makes it so the actual webpage isn't downloaded, only the headers. Check that the website doesnt block HEAD requests though

> i don't want to pass to generic bcoz it's bloated.

redirect is the first thing GenericIE tries
```py
        self.to_screen('%s: Requesting header' % video_id)

        head_req = HEADRequest(url)
        head_response = self._request_webpage(
            head_req, video_id,
            note=False, errnote='Could not send HEAD request to %s' % url,
            fatal=False)

        if head_response is not False:
            # Check for redirect
            new_url = head_response.geturl()
            if url != new_url:
                self.report_following_redirect(new_url)
                if force_videoid:
                    new_url = smuggle_url(
                        new_url, {'force_videoid': force_videoid})
                return self.url_result(new_url)
```

but your wish. Either method is fine

body:
tube.tugraz.at has many lectures that have both a camera stream from the lecture hall ("PRESENTER"), showing the lecturer and the blackboard, as well as a video stream from the connected computer ("PRESENTATION"), usually showing a powerpoint presentation or similar.

The majority of lecture videos uses only PRESENTATION streams, or has a PRESENTER stream that doesn't really show anything useful, and most lecturers that use the blackboard don't use a PRESENTATION stream at all.

These are two separate video streams that can be watched side-by-side on the website if both are present. As far as I understand it, it's hard to map this to YT-DLP's model, so making a choice of which stream to choose by default is necessary if consistent behaviour is desired.

Choosing the best PRESENTATION stream by default is most likely the most sane option, because it will almost always deliver a video that actually contains the content you want to watch. In cases where you want a specific stream, you can still choose.
author: Ferdi265

body:
IMO this is necessary if users actually want to know what they are downloading. (see the note about `preference`)

Here is a comparison of formats with and without `_gen_format_id()` (for video `https://tube.tugraz.at/paella/ui/watch.html?id=6b78b5ef-605d-423c-9b6b-5e6260121f26`):

With format ids:
```
presenter-dash-abr129      m4a        audio only  [eng] DASH audio,  129k, m4a_dash container, mp4a.40.2@129k (44100Hz), ~83.55GiB
presenter-dash-abr162      m4a        audio only  [eng] DASH audio,  162k, m4a_dash container, mp4a.40.2@162k (44100Hz), ~104.92GiB
presenter-dash-abr196      m4a        audio only  [eng] DASH audio,  196k, m4a_dash container, mp4a.40.2@196k (44100Hz), ~126.53GiB
presenter-dash-vbr2134     mp4        768x432     DASH video, 2134k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d401e@2134k, 30fps, video only, ~1.35TiB
presenter-dash-vbr2150     mp4        1280x720    DASH video, 2150k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d401f@2150k, 30fps, video only, ~1.36TiB
presenter-dash-vbr2267     mp4        1920x1080   DASH video, 2267k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d4028@2267k, 30fps, video only, ~1.43TiB
presenter-https-br2134     mp4        768x432     2134kvideo@2004k, 30fps, audio@129k, ~1.35TiB
presenter-hls-br2134       mp4        768x432     2134kvideo@2134k, audio@  0k, ~1.35TiB
presenter-https-br2150     mp4        1280x720    2150kvideo@1988k, 30fps, audio@162k, ~1.36TiB
presenter-hls-br2150       mp4        1280x720    2150kvideo@2150k, audio@  0k, ~1.36TiB
presenter-https-br2267     mp4        1920x1080   2267kvideo@2071k, 30fps, audio@196k, ~1.43TiB
presenter-hls-br2267       mp4        1920x1080   2267kvideo@2267k, audio@  0k, ~1.43TiB
presentation-dash-vbr270   mp4        512x320     DASH video,  270k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d4015@ 270k, 30fps, video only, ~174.28GiB
presentation-dash-vbr481   mp4        852x532     DASH video,  481k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d401f@ 481k, 30fps, video only, ~310.53GiB
presentation-dash-vbr699   mp4        1280x800    DASH video,  699k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d4020@ 699k, 30fps, video only, ~451.63GiB
presentation-https-vbr270  mp4        512x320      270k, 30fps
presentation-https-vbr481  mp4        852x532      481k, 30fps
presentation-https-vbr699  mp4        1280x800     699k, 30fps
presentation-hls-br270     mp4        512x320      270kvideo@ 270k, audio@  0k, ~174.28GiB
presentation-hls-br481     mp4        852x532      481kvideo@ 481k, audio@  0k, ~310.53GiB
presentation-hls-br699     mp4        1280x800     699kvideo@ 699k, audio@  0k, ~451.63GiB
```

Without format ids:
```
p0aa0br129446   m4a        audio only  [eng] DASH audio,  129k, m4a_dash container, mp4a.40.2@129k (44100Hz), ~83.55GiB
p0aa0br162563   m4a        audio only  [eng] DASH audio,  162k, m4a_dash container, mp4a.40.2@162k (44100Hz), ~104.92GiB
p0aa0br196045   m4a        audio only  [eng] DASH audio,  196k, m4a_dash container, mp4a.40.2@196k (44100Hz), ~126.53GiB
p0va0br2134088  mp4        768x432     DASH video, 2134k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d401e@2134k, 30fps, video only, ~1.35TiB
p0va0br2150658  mp4        1280x720    DASH video, 2150k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d401f@2150k, 30fps, video only, ~1.36TiB
p0va0br2267088  mp4        1920x1080   DASH video, 2267k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d4028@2267k, 30fps, video only, ~1.43TiB
6               mp4        768x432     2134kvideo@2004k, 30fps, audio@129k, ~1.35TiB
2134            mp4        768x432     2134kvideo@2134k, audio@  0k, ~1.35TiB
8               mp4        1280x720    2150kvideo@1988k, 30fps, audio@162k, ~1.36TiB
2150            mp4        1280x720    2150kvideo@2150k, audio@  0k, ~1.36TiB
10              mp4        1920x1080   2267kvideo@2071k, 30fps, audio@196k, ~1.43TiB
2267            mp4        1920x1080   2267kvideo@2267k, audio@  0k, ~1.43TiB
p0va0br270028   mp4        512x320     DASH video,  270k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d4015@ 270k, 30fps, video only, ~174.28GiB
p0va0br481131   mp4        852x532     DASH video,  481k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d401f@ 481k, 30fps, video only, ~310.53GiB
p0va0br699756   mp4        1280x800    DASH video,  699k, mp4_dash container, avc1.4d4020@ 699k, 30fps, video only, ~451.63GiB
15              mp4        512x320      270k, 30fps
16              mp4        852x532      481k, 30fps
17              mp4        1280x800     699k, 30fps
270             mp4        512x320      270kvideo@ 270k, audio@  0k, ~174.28GiB
481             mp4        852x532      481kvideo@ 481k, audio@  0k, ~310.53GiB
699             mp4        1280x800     699kvideo@ 699k, audio@  0k, ~451.63GiB
```

It's almost impossible to discern whether a format is a PRESENTATION or PRESENTER format without labelling them.

Additionally, some transports seem to be slower to download than others (the full video downloads over https usually go far quicker than the chunked HLS or DASH downloads, at least in my experience).

Also, please disregard the sizes listed next to the formats. I know they are completely wrong, I'm currently trying to figure out what causes them to be off.

> Is there a cleaner way to keep the hardsubs from getting selected as default? (I doubt very many people ever want the hardsubbed versions, given that softsubs are provided)

yes, making it not selected by default is easy. But that won't reduce the http requests

> Should I just ignore hardsubs generally? Those extra http requests slow down the extractor and are probably almost never needed.

I'm sure someone will ask for it. And since the extractor args already exist for CrunchyrollIE, might as well do the same here
author: pukkandan

body:
One potential wrinkle here. The beta api uses language tags with dashes (`en-US`), rather than without (`enUS`) like the old system. Should we provide compatibility with the extractor args for crunchyroll here or match the api?

For that matter, should we be using the same extractor args as `CrunchyrollIE` or use a separate key for beta?
author: tejing1

body:
Imo, it is best to use the same key and maintain compat since it's just a matter of doing `.replace('-', '')`.

But I'll leave the decision to your discretion. I dont use the extractor, and you are in a better position to judge whether users would want to apply the same key to it or want the flexibility of separate keys
author: pukkandan

body:
In that case, the language codes used for the extractor args wouldn't match those used for subtitles, unless we converted those too.

Which I'm against, since the new system follows standards (RFC 5646), so we probably shouldn't be porting back to non-standardized tags just for backward compatibility.

Using yt-dlp for anime downloading across multiple sites already involves handling the language tags on a per-site basis, so I don't think changing them as part of the beta site handling is a big hassle to users.

Also, the `language` extractor arg from `CrunchyrollIE` doesn't actually apply here, as audio language is fixed (at an api level) for a given video id (dubbed versions of content fall under their own season ids and episode ids), and it makes sense to implement an extractor arg to choose which stream types to retrieve, so I think I'm going to use a separate key string for beta.
author: tejing1

body: Both options should default to extracting all formats/subs. It is more important to make all data available by default than to reduce a few network requests. The extractor-arg can be used to limit it
author: pukkandan

body:
As a user, I'd much prefer the hardsubs not show up normally, since they're basically never what you want, and it's a waste to go fetch them all.

And do you really want the default form of the extractor to make 45 requests? Serially? Taking 9 seconds in my example test? And end up with 400 formats? Many of which are precisely identical data just coming from different servers?

I mean, you're the boss, but I don't think this is a good idea. At the very least, it should probably advertise the extractor args clearly in the output, because otherwise a lot of people will just sit through this, not realizing it could be way faster. Indirect users, like those using it through `mpv`s hook script, won't even see that though :-/

body:
Because It gets that from the first item of Json. ([fc_facts][0][id], or fc_created) it works when they are immutable. 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/216170/152388217-88745a0e-44e9-476e-83c9-6b933bfcd809.png)

But for some videos this items changes. And we don't get a reliable information.

For example:
Yesterday the first item of the same video (https://beeg.com/-0599050563103750) had the id 2569975.
Today the first item is 2569969, and the 2569975 is on second item.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/216170/152390120-8113cc76-fa1c-4f4a-8091-5d6c96768b9a.png)

I removed it from return because tests keeps failing for the same correct video.

If there is a way to ignore these keys from tests and keep it in return, it is ok to bring it back.

body:
All this can be converted to a list comprehension at the cost of readability, something like
```py
formats = [{ 'format_note': f'{t} stream',
            'url': str_or_none(data.get(f'{t}Mp4Url')),
            'width': int_or_none(data.get(f'{t}ResolvtionsWidth')),
            'height': int_or_none(data.get(f'{t}ResolvtionsHeight')),
            'format_id': str_or_none(data.get('recordingId') if t == 'view' else data.get('shareVideoId')),
            'ext': 'mp4',
            'filesize_approx': parse_filesize(data.get('fileSize')),} for t in ['view', 'share']]
```
If u don't want to do it then add a check for `viewMp4Url` like `if data.get('viewMp4Url'):` at line 74
author: Ashish0804

body:
the `filesize_approx` is only for the `view` and not the `share`, I checked and `share` does not have an equivalent
and its important for the `share` to be first, because then its the default when not specifying format and thus keeping backward compatibility (that the view is downloaded)
and the list comprehension does not check if the matching `url` exist, zoom always sends the field `shareMp4Url` for example, but its empty on videos without screen share

if the `viewMp4Url` then nothing else will, this is the main video and I haven't seen a zoom recording without it
the previous version did not check it, and it makes sense because this is the only thing it downloaded
I added the check for the `shareMp4Url` because it doesn't exist on videos that so not share screen
it makes more sense to throw an error if `viewMp4Url` doesn't exist

```suggestion
            r'<script[^<]+?window.globEmbedUrl\s*=\s*\'((?:https?:)?//(?:daxab\.com|dxb\.to|[^/]+/player)/[^\']+)\'',
```
I think this is causing the need for dotall. In general, we want to avoid `.` completely in order to prevent issues like catastrophic backtracking and poor performance on large pages. So needing dotall often means there is some issue in the regex

```suggestion def _real_extract(self, url): video_id, tmp_id, display_id = self._match_valid_url(url).group('id', 'tmp_id', 'display_id') webpage = self._download_webpage(url, video_id or tmp_id) if not tmp_id: video_id = self._html_search_regex( r'src="https?://videocampus\.sachsen\.de/media/embed\?key=([0-9a-f]+)&', webpage, 'video_id') title = self._html_search_regex( (r'

([^<]+)

', r']+name=(["\'])title\1[^>]+content=\1(?P(?:(?!\1).)+)\1[^>]*/>'), webpage, 'title') formats, subtitles = self._extract_m3u8_formats_and_subtitles( f'https://videocampus.sachsen.de/media/hlsMedium/key/{video_id}/format/auto/ext/mp4/learning/0/path/m3u8', video_id, 'mp4', 'm3u8_native', m3u8_id='hls') self._sort_formats(formats) return { 'id': video_id, 'title': title, 'display_id': display_id, 'formats': formats, 'subtitles': subtitles } class VideocampusSachsenEmbedIE(InfoExtractor): _VALID_URL = r'https?://videocampus.sachsen.de/media/embed\?key=(?P[0-9a-f]+)' _TESTS = [ { 'url': 'https://videocampus.sachsen.de/media/embed?key=fc99c527e4205b121cb7c74433469262', 'info_dict': { 'id': 'fc99c527e4205b121cb7c74433469262', 'title': 'Was ist selbstgesteuertes Lernen?', 'ext': 'mp4', }, } ] def _real_extract(self, url): video_id = self._match_id(url) webpage = self._download_webpage(url, video_id) title = self._html_search_regex(r']*title="([^"<]+)"', webpage, 'title') formats, subtitles = self._extract_m3u8_formats_and_subtitles( f'https://videocampus.sachsen.de/media/hlsMedium/key/{video_id}/format/auto/ext/mp4/learning/0/path/m3u8', video_id, 'mp4', 'm3u8_native', m3u8_id='hls') self._sort_formats(formats) return { 'id': video_id, 'title': title, 'formats': formats, 'subtitles': subtitles, } ```

body:
An easier way to do this is:
```py
@functools.cached_property
def method(self):
    return 'GET' if self.data is None else 'POST'
```
Then `req.method = 'POST'` will automatically override this property. No need to have a separate `_method` or setter

body:
Keep in mind that this technically allows:
```py
req = HEADRequest(...)
req.method = 'GET'
assert isinstance(req, HEADRequest)  # Will pass
```
I think it's better to have it be a function instead. Then it's clear that it doesn't support instance checking
```py
def HEADRequest(*args, **kwargs):
    return Request(*args, **kwargs, method='HEAD')
```
or just
```py
HEADRequest = functools.partial(Request, method='HEAD')
```

body:
can u explain?
```py
>>> import collections
>>> class CaseInsensitiveDict(collections.UserDict):
...     def __setitem__(self, key, value):
...         super().__setitem__(key.title(), str(value))
...
...     def __getitem__(self, key):
...         return super().__getitem__(key.title())
...
...     def __delitem__(self, key):
...         super().__delitem__(key.title())
...
...     def __contains__(self, key):
...         if not isinstance(key, str):
...             return False
...         return super().__contains__(key.title())
...
>>>
>>> h = CaseInsensitiveDict(Abcd=1, efGh=2)
>>> print(h)
{'Abcd': '1', 'Efgh': '2'}
>>>
>>> assert h == CaseInsensitiveDict({'ABCD': 1, 'EFGH': 2})  # eq
>>> assert 'ABCD' in h  # contains
>>>
>>> h2 = h.copy()  # copy
>>> h2['AbCd'] = 3
>>> assert h['abcd'] == '1'
>>>
>>> assert h | h2 == h2  # or
>>>
>>> h.update({'aBCd': 3})  # update
>>> assert h == h2
```

body:
> I was just reading through here: [https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.10/Lib/collections/__init__.py#L1125-L1144](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.10/Lib/collections/__init__.py?rgh-link-date=2022-06-27T01%3A51%3A40Z#L1125-L1144)

> Wouldn't it do OR with our titled() dictionary and whatever the other one is? unless I am misunderstanding something

It first `or`'s them as dicts. But when it is cast to `CaseInsensitiveDict`, the duplicate entries are resolved

The whole point of `UserDict` is that it makes inheriting from dict easy. If we had to redefine everything, there is no point :D

body:
Having a better look, it's not too bad as I initially thought. More that there is a lack of sanitization in some areas, but not that serious. It's out of scope of this networking rework project but something that could be improved upon sometime.

some things from a quick read:
- only FFmpegFD uses handle_youtubedl_headers to strip accept-encoding header (and the no compression internal header). Other downloaders leave both in and leak to server.
- Other internal headers will be leaked if used (though not much of a worry since they are rarely used)
-  passing proxies is only supported by a few external downloaders (and if they don't they will use no proxy without any sort of notice to user)
     - this is also an issue with current websocket support
- external downloaders that do support proxies generally just take it from ydl opts 
    - ignoring env proxies, though depends on if the external downloader will pick those up itself
- FFmpegFD proxy sanitization will create a bad proxy url if you use a scheme-less url like `//example.com:1080`
- Other external downloaders don't do any proxy validation/sanitization
- yt-dlp doesn't support `socks5h` scheme, rather it treats `socks5` scheme as `socks5h` (use remote DNS). Downloaders that do separate the two will treat the socks5 proxy as the one that uses local DNS (i.e. potential unwanted DNS leak)
- user passes proxy supported by yt-dlp but not by the external downloader
author: coletdjnz

body:
I'm not entirely confident in the design of this request flow.

There should to be a way to check if a handler can support a request or not (without it making the actual request if it does support).

Originally I had a `can_handle` function that returned True/False for this.
But this had issues:
- no easy way of getting the reason why the handler is not supported
- the request needs to be "modified" in a way before it can be supported.
    - we may find out while preparing the request that it is not supported
   
So then I went with a prepare request function then handle flow

The situation we have now:
- we have this weird public prepare_request function that modifies the given Request.. with no use of the modification outside the RH
- handle() function which calls prepare_request function internally to get the modified Request

One way I was thinking we could clean this up could be by doing:
- make prepare_request internal only
- Have a `can_handle` function that runs a given Request through prepare_request, but doesn't return a Request
    - when it can't handle, do we want to raise the UnsupportedRequest error with a reason or return a bool?

but I am open to other suggestions too
author: coletdjnz

body:
I feel like this is overcomplicated, we can use a couple modifications to simplify this:
```py
    @classmethod
    def build(cls, errors: dict[Handler, Exception]):
        message = 'Cannot handle request'

        if errors:
            error_map = defaultdict(list)
            for handler, error in errors.items():
                message = error.msg if isinstance(error, UnsupportedRequest) else 'Unexpected error'
                error_map[message].append(handler.RH_NAME)

            reasons = ', '.join(f'{msg} ({", ".join(handlers)})' for msg, handlers in error_map.items())
            message = f'{message}: {reasons}'

        return cls(msg=message)
```
Using a `build` classmethod seems cleaner as it doesnt violate Liskov.
Which will produce the following output:

`Cannot handle request: Unsupported url scheme "file" (urllib, requests), Unexpected error (httpx)`

This would require a slight modification of the director to store a dict, and the classes (classes, not instances) to be hashable.

body:
I prefer to have it strict like this, and YoutubeDL is the one that figures that out imo. I'm not really a fan of that pattern from the FDs etc having that delegated to the handler. I treat the handlers like individual clients with their own set of parameters, build on from what they inherit.

- If it is a parameter common to all handlers (i.e may be supported by all), then it is a part of the base RequestHandler.
- If it is a parameter common to a subset of handlers (e.g. Websocket handlers), then it is a part of that subclass.
- If it is a parameter is only for a specific handler (e.g. urllib), then it only gets passed to that handler.

There shouldn't be many cases of having additional parameters outside of handler subsets like websockets.
author: coletdjnz

- print debug logging from urllib3 if debutraffic is set (urllib3 uses the `logging` module)

body:
this is done to handle `your_dict['key']` being literally `None`. It's applicable not just for iterators, but anywhere None or empty string can cause issues. While this specific case is not stated in contributing, similar situations are mentioned

---

Another thing to remember is not to try to iterate over `None`

Say you extracted a list of thumbnails into `thumbnail_data` using `try_get` and now want to iterate over them

```python
thumbnail_data = try_get(...)
thumbnails = [{
    'url': item['url']
} for item in thumbnail_data or []]  # correct
```

and not like:

```python
thumbnail_data = try_get(...)
thumbnails = [{
    'url': item['url']
} for item in thumbnail_data]  # incorrect
```

In the later case, `thumbnail_data` will be `None` if the field was not found and this will cause the loop `for item in thumbnail_data` to raise a fatal error. Using `for item in thumbnail_data or []` avoids this error and results in setting an empty list in `thumbnails` instead.

---


Assume that you have some another source you can extract `title` from, for example `og:title` HTML meta of a `webpage`. In this case you can provide a fallback scenario:

```python
title = meta.get('title') or self._og_search_title(webpage)
```

This code will try to extract from `meta` first and if it fails it will try extracting `og:title` from a `webpage`.

I should have worded my original comment better.
I didn't mean to say that make the actual call once.
What i meant was,

That from my understanding of this code, the API requires a cookie to be set before it starts sending u actual data.

So how this works rn (As i understand it)
1. u make a req at endpoint 1
2. it makes an if check for the cookie flag
3. it makes dummy req at endpoint 1
4. it sets that cookie flag to be true
5. it makes actual data request at endpoint 1
6. u make req at endpoint 2
7. it makes an if check for that cookie flag
8. it makes actual data request at endpoint 2
and so on...

What I'm suggesting is, eliminate this cookie flag check and do

1. make a req for the cookie
2. make actual data req for endpoint1.
3. make actual data req for endpoint2.
and so on

body:
```suggestion
        video_id = self._match_id(url)
        src_iframe = self._search_regex(
            r'\<iframe[^>]+src="([^"]+)"', self._download_webpage(url, video_id), 'src iframe')

        webpage_iframe = self._download_webpage(
            src_iframe.replace('player.php', 'config2.php'), video_id, headers={'Referer': src_iframe})

        sources = self._parse_json(self._search_regex(
            r'sources\:[^>](\[.*?\])', webpage_iframe, 'm3u8 source'), video_id, transform_source=js_to_json)

        formats, subtitles = [], {}
        for source in sources:
            file = source.get('file')
            if file and source.get('type') == 'm3u8':
                fmts, subs = self._extract_m3u8_formats_and_subtitles(
                    urljoin('https://www.lightcast.com/embed/', file),
                    video_id, 'mp4', fatal=False, headers={'Referer': src_iframe})
                formats.extend(fmts)
                subtitles = self._merge_subtitles(subtitles, subs)
        self._sort_formats(formats)
        
        return {
            'id': video_id,

            'subtitles': subtitles,
```

body:
Probably. It can't happen now, but future changes to crunchyroll might cause it. I'm not sure how best to actually store the necessary state, though.

Or perhaps it should be done without state? The non-beta extractor could refuse to switch and raise an error instead if not logged in.
author: tejing1

body:
Yes, most of the metadata is available at this point. I wasn't aware you could short-circuit the extractor using partial metadata with match_filter that way. I should go read that code...

You'd still end up fetching the episode list for every season, but given the general overhead of dealing with a playlist that's probably not too big a deal.
author: tejing1

body: The standard way to bind a state to a URL is to do `url = smuggle_url(url, state_dict)`. Then the receiving extractor can do `url, smuggled_data = unsmuggle_url(url)`

body: Should this actually be `self`? If an inheriting class were to override `params` it would behave rather badly. I think it should uniformly access the variable via the class name, not mix up both kinds of reference.

author: P-reducible

body:
You can use `\s` or `\b`:
```
r'<script\s[^>]*?\bsrc\s*=\s*"(?P<path>/static/js/[^">]+)"[^>]*>'
r'<script\b[^>]*?\bsrc\s*=\s*"(?P<path>/static/js/[^">]+)"[^>]*>'
```
No point using `\s+` as `[^>]` matches whitespace. Also, you presumably want the `path` group not to match just `/static/js/`, and to be the value of a `src` attribute rather than say `notasrc`; the above patterns match, eg, `... data-src="/static/js/abc.js" ...`: if you only want `... src=...` to match:
```
r'<script\b[^>]*?(?<!\w|-)src\s*=\s*"(?P<path>/static/js/[^">]+)"[^>]*>'
```
 Finally, you could also allow for single quotes:
```
r'''<script\b[^>]*?\bsrc\s*=\s*(?P<q>"|')(?P<path>/static/js/(?:(?!(?P=q))[^>])+)(?P=q)[^>]*>'''
```

body:
@pukkandan I don't see anything wrong with the question mark here. Should the corrected version be

`
'<script\s+[^>]*?\s+src\s*=\s*"(?P<path>/static/js/[^">]+)"\s+[^>]*>'
`

? This would match `'<script attr1 = "val1" attr2 src  = "/static/js/file.js" attr3="val3"  >'` but not `'<script attr1 = "val1" attr2src  = "/static/js/file.js" attr3="val3"  >'` and not `'<script attr1 = "val1" attr2 src  = "/static/js/" attr3="val3"  >'`.

If you want, I can remove the trailing `'\s+[^>]*>'` part which I only added for robustness sake.

body:
It's not quite identical - in one call, the param is `episode_id` while in the other, it's `ep_id`. I don't like the repetition either, but I also don't want to have a flag argument controlling "do we use the long or the short param name?".

I think the way I'd like to be able to write this code is with a helper to exclude `None` from dicts, so that the similar-but-not-identical dicts can just be created inline. Still somewhat repetitive, but the repeated section is smaller/cleaner:

```
    self._call_api(
        ...,
        query=exclude_none({
            'platform': 'web',
            'episode_id': ep_id,
            'aid': aid,
        }))
...

def exclude_none(d: dict):
    return {k: v for k, v in d.items() if v is not None}
```

What are your thoughts on a helper like that? I think I've seen this kind of conditional-add-to-dict logic enough that it would make sense for something in utils.py.

body:
Unfortunately, `self._html_search_meta` is too broad, at least for the `description` field.

For example, in the Interstellar wegpage the following two entities are present:
```
<meta name="description" content="Interstellar Trailer & Teaser, Interviews, Clips und mehr Videos auf Deutsch und im Original. Schaue dir alle 35 Videos jetzt an!"> 
```
```
<meta itemprop="description" content="16 von 35 (Englisch, 2014), Originaltitle: Interstellar, Hard SF, Science Fiction-Film, Abenteuerfilm, von Christopher Nolan, mit Matthew McConaughey, Anne Hathaway" />
```

My function matches the (correct) second one, while `self._html_search_meta` matches also the first one.

title is mandatory in the sense that extractors must return it. But returning None as title is allowed in yt-dlp. In this case, `YoutubeDL` will construct a generic title https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/5fa3c9a88f597625296981a4a26be723e65d4842/CONTRIBUTING.md?plain=1#L261

```
❯ yt-dlp -O title= https://www.tiktok.com/@pokemonlife22/video/7059698374567611694
WARNING: Extractor failed to obtain "title". Creating a generic title instead
title = 'TikTok video #7059698374567611694'
```
author: pukkandan

body:
Although I've always been somewhat sceptical about yt-dl's mandatory `title`, I can't help wondering why it's important that `info_dict['title']` should work even if `info_dict.get('title') is None`.

Also s/it's/its/ in https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/5fa3c9a88f597625296981a4a26be723e65d4842/CONTRIBUTING.md#L261!
author: dirkf

body: I believe this decision was originally made because the filename is derived from title (before `-o` was a thing). If you are asking why I decided to make yt-dlp generate a title instead of making it optional; the main reason is to maintain compatibility (in the sense that all videos have a title). As a bonus, this ensures that the default output template is somewhat sensible even when title extraction fails. I got the idea of `{extractor} video #{id}` from the facebook extractor
author: pukkandan

body: All understandable, but why not generate the title for the `'title' not in info_dict` case too?
author: dirkf

body: Because I can't think of a good reason why an extractor shouldn't at least try to return a proper title. I wanted to make make downloads work even when title extraction fails, but keep it as a mandatory field that the extractor should always return
author: pukkandan

body: Another reason (to make missing `title` non-fatal): an extractor doesn't know if it's being invoked via `url_transparent` with a valid `title` already set from the invoking page.

author: dirkf

body: You have misspelt ‘disadvantage’. Masking errors, especially when extracting the most crucial datum, the video URL, is a bug, pure and simple, that should be surfaced as early as possible, reported and eventually fixed. Even PHP is moving away from the PHP school of error handling: pretend nothing happened and hope for the best (okay, fine, `ON ERROR RESUME NEXT` is even older than PHP).
author: fstirlitz

body:
"possible" vs "dis-"
If the structure were controlled by us, I agree. But one novel item structure shouldn't break whatever extraction still works: maybe a WARNING, though. If no video URLs are found at all then a crash is quite reasonable and the `_sort_formats()` method provides it.

body:
I'm not sure I like the side effect of this function moving along the timer if it would return true. If you just want to check if it has elapsed, but act apon it later this would prevent that. I'd rather have it be explicit.

For example, `if self.timer.has_elapsed(seconds=self.UPDATE_TIMESPAN_S):` from `Speedometer` calls it, moving the timer forward, but that shouldn't be happening right?
author: Grub4K

body:
Even in the case of a late call, we would want have the next call be the set time apart and not moved closer
```
Norm    |    |    |    |    |
Current |    |    |      |  |
Desired |    |    |      |    |
```
This ensures we have at minimum x seconds inbetween. But I guess it could be argued for either side. Why did you choose to do it this way?
author: Grub4K

body: Maybe use `math.inf` instead? Maybe it's just me that doesn't like `float('inf')`
author: Grub4K

body: Instead of using `max(0, bisect - 1)`, how about using `bisect_left`?
author: Grub4K

body: This whole bisecting seems weird when you can just use deque instead?
author: Jules-A

body:
`bisect` is used to find the place where we have to threat the values as old values.
Using a `deque` works for storing the data as well, but then you have to call `deque.popleft` in a loop. This might end up faster however and could be considered.

It can be argued that bisecting this problem itself is overkill. We can use simple iteration from the front, since we have 0-2 elements to remove doing that should be about equally as fast.
author: Grub4K

body:
> `bisect` is used to find the place where we have to threat the values as old values. Using a `deque` works for storing the data as well, but then you have to call `deque.popleft` in a loop. 

Nah, just adding a value clears the first value if it's at the storage limit.
author: Jules-A

body: We want to have an accurate representation over "time", not over datapoints. Datapoints could be very close together, or very far apart (in case of fluctuating internet) which is why we store an arbitrary amount of datapoints based on time
author: Grub4K

body: It is over time though and it doesn't change anything if you decide to use deque instead.
author: Jules-A

body:
> Community posts and video comment replies may show up as notifications, but they should be ignored. It may be possible to extract from those posts/replies, but that was beyond my original goal for this new feature.

Nothing to be done about comments, but we could extract posts since posts can contain embedded videos

> Since notifications can be practically endless, the extractor keyword has a value argument that specifies the maximum number of pages to fetch (each page has 20 real entries), with a sane default of 1 (i.e. no continuation). I haven't tested to see how far back it goes for fear of throttling, but I can fetch 20 pages (~400 entries) in 4 seconds.

No need for any artificial limitations/extra parameters. As long as the extractor yields the results lazily, `--playlist-end` will handle the rest

> Dates are relative and imprecise (e.g. "1 day ago") and may also be plainly inaccurate. The action in the message text (e.g. "uploaded", "is live") is not accurate (a livestream vod that was unprivated long after the stream ended would show as "uploaded", for example).

We generally do not want to return inaccurate fields. But For `upload_date`, obey the `approximate_date` extractor-arg

> The included thumbnail is weird: for livestreams only, the height is set to 0. 

We should remove the empty thumbnail imo. But returning it is technically fine

> There is also the issue that without flat-playlist, this thumbnail is removed from the infodict's list of thumbnails entirely.

That is expected behavior. The thumbnails we get from video extraction is more accurate information


PS: Some of my review comments are inter-related. Pls read all of them before starting to make changes

body:
since the playlist is unviewable, we can't use the /playlist page.

playnext=1 will automatically make it redirect to the watch page of the first video in the playlist, and then we extract using the watch page "inline" playlist

i.e. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=chHM75fI770&list=RLTDwFCb4jeqaKWnciAYM-ZVHg

author: Lesmiscore

body: If the stream comes in multiple codecs, some qns might not be available in other codecs. E.g. `avc` and `hevc` both provide 10000 but 150 is only present in `avc` codec, in this case the API would give us the URL for `hevc` with 10000 qn. To avoid duplicates, we discard the unwanted formats and do them in their own API request.

Swings and roundabouts:
```diff
diff --git a/yt_dlp/extractor/safari.py b/yt_dlp/extractor/safari.py
index af32f9777..fcfc0159c 100644
--- a/yt_dlp/extractor/safari.py
+++ b/yt_dlp/extractor/safari.py
@@ -124,12 +124,14 @@ def _real_extract(self, url):
             if reference_id:
                 query['flashvars[referenceId]'] = reference_id
             else:
-                try:
-                    query['flashvars[referenceId]'] = self._search_regex(
-                        r'data-reference-id=(["\'])(?P<id>(?:(?!\1).)+)\1',
-                        webpage, 'kaltura reference id', group='id')
-                except RegexNotFoundError as e:
-                    self.report_warning(e.orig_msg)
+                _name = 'kaltura reference id'
+                reference_id = self._search_regex(
+                    r'data-reference-id=(["\'])(?P<id>(?:(?!\1).)+)\1',
+                    webpage, _name, default=None, group='id')
+                if reference_id is not None:
+                    query['flashvars[referenceId]'] = reference_id
+                else:
+                    self.report_warning('Unable to extract %s' % _name)
                     query['entry_id'] = self._search_regex(
                         r'data-entry-id=(["\'])(?P<id>(?:(?!\1).)+)\1',
                         webpage, 'kaltura entry id', group='id')
```

body:
```suggestion
        video_id = self._match_id(url)
        webpage = self._download_webpage(url, video_id)
        streams_data = self._download_json(self._extract_js_var(webpage, 'tokenURL'), video_id)

        formats, subtitles = [], {}
        for stream_url in traverse_obj(streams_data, (
                'response', 'data', lambda k, _: k.startswith('content')), expected_type=url_or_none):
            fmts, subs = self._extract_m3u8_formats_and_subtitles(stream_url, video_id, 'mp4', m3u8_id='hls', live=True)
            formats.extend(fmts)
            subtitles = self._merge_subtitles(subtitles, subs)
        self._sort_formats(formats)

        stream_title = self._extract_js_var(webpage, 'video_title', 'LRT')
        return {
            'id': video_id,
            'formats': formats,
            'subtitles': subtitles,
            'is_live': True,
            'title': f'{self._og_search_title(webpage)} - {stream_title}'
        }
```

body:
The URLs I get from the website always seem to be single episodes, so people who want to download entire shows/seasons will probably have to use `--print series_id` or `--print season_id` anyway.

While manually constructed season/series URLs seem to be valid, they just start playing the first episode on the website (while listing others as well), and it would require up to 3 API calls per link to guess if something is a movie/episode, season or series. I can implement that if desired, but it's probably not worth it unless the website starts actually using such URLs.

body:
might be a good idea to collect submodules just in case:
- if `requests` or `urllib3` have any hidden imports of their own they will be missed by pyinstaller without this
- if new version of pyinst changes something
- if user is building with older version of pyinst
```py
    # Only `websockets` is required, others are collected just in case
    for module in ('websockets', 'requests', 'urllib3'):
        yield from collect_submodules(module)
    # These are auto-detected, but explicitly add them just in case
    yield from ('mutagen', 'brotli', 'certifi')
```

author: hheimbuerger

body:
I don't. But it isn't the first time. I remember that this happened before, that I returned to this extractor/platform after a couple of months and suddenly all MD5s had changed.

Maybe they sometime reencode the videos? Or the metadata is embedded into the MD5, and the metadata schema changes over time?

Either way, they don't change on a daily or weekly basis, but they seem to change every couple of months.
author: hheimbuerger

body:
Makes sense!

For the record, this requires
```
{
    "usenetrc": true
}
```
in the file `test/local_parameters.json`. Specifying `username` and `password` directly doesn't work, because it would be supplied to the Zype extractor as well, and that can't take any.
author: hheimbuerger

body:
Yeah, this was not a well thought-out change.

Originally, the number was arbitrarily set. It wasn't even to test pagination (wasn't it back then anyway requesting all pages?), I just did not know what the consequences are. Now I noticed that this causes the test cases to request all 100 videos, and I thought I could speed them up (barely!) by reducing this number.

I changed it back. I guess testing pagination makes sense here and the extra second(s) the test takes don't really matter.
author: hheimbuerger

body: Even though that signature is currently never called, I think it's valid for this function to be called without explicit credentials. In that case, it only attempts authentication via cookies and only if those cannot be found, `raise_login_required()` will be raised.

body:
**Two things here**:

1/ It looks like this [`break` statement](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/yt_dlp/extractor/common.py#L1505=) has the unfortunate consequence that when several `json_ld`s are found, if one of them is not top level and `traverse_json_ld` is called recursively, subsequent `json_ld`s are skipped. Is that on purpose?

- If yes, this implementation of `traverse_json_ld` doesn't seem to be compatible with what's required for this extractor: the first `json_ld` is not top level, and the relevant information is in the second one the parsing of which never happens because of this `break`.
- If not, the `break` can be replaced by a `continue` to parse the remaining `json_ld`s, but IMHO it's beyond the scope of this PR, and I hit a second problem anyway:

2/ Looks like `common.py`'s `traverse_json_ld` has built-in support for [`TVEpisode`, `Episode`](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/yt_dlp/extractor/common.py#L1512=), [`Movie`](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/yt_dlp/extractor/common.py#L1530=), [`Article`, `NewsArticle`](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/yt_dlp/extractor/common.py#L1537=) and [`VideoObject`](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/yt_dlp/extractor/common.py#L1547=). If it's not one of them, then [a last attempt is made to extract the video](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/yt_dlp/extractor/common.py#L1553-L1555=).

However, this particular `json_ld` that's relevant for this extractor is a `RadioEpisode`, the content of which is the following:

```json
{
  "@type": "RadioEpisode",
  "name": "La physique d’Einstein aiderait-elle à comprendre le cerveau ?",
  "description": "Existerait-il un pont conceptuel entre la physique de l’espace-temps et les neurosciences ?",
  "dateCreated": "2022-05-14T14:00:00.000Z",
  "inLanguage": "fr",
  "isAccessibleForFree": "True",
  "mainEntity": {
    "@type": "AudioObject",
    "contentUrl": "https://media.radiofrance-podcast.net/podcast09/23118-14.05.2022-ITEMA_23026189-2022C22785S0134-21.mp3",
    "duration": "P0Y0M0DT0H45M50S",
    "encodingFormat": "mp3",
    "potentialAction": {
      "@type": "Action",
      "name": "ListenAction"
    }
  },
  "image": {
    "@type": "ImageObject",
    "url": "https://cdn.radiofrance.fr/s3/cruiser-production/2022/05/d184e7a3-4827-4494-bf94-04ed7b120db4/2048x640_gettyimages-200171095-001.jpg",
    "width": "1891",
    "height": "590",
    "copyrightHolder": "Getty"
  },
  "director": [
    {
      "@type": "Person",
      "name": "Etienne Klein"
    }
  ],
  "partOfSeries": {
    "@type": "RadioSeries",
    "name": "La physique d’Einstein aiderait-elle à comprendre le cerveau ?",
    "url": "https://www.radiofrance.fr/franceculture/podcasts/science-en-questions/la-physique-d-einstein-aiderait-elle-a-comprendre-le-cerveau-8440487"
  }
}
```

Not being one of the supported type, and not having a `video` key, leads the parsing reach the next [break statement](https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/master/yt_dlp/extractor/common.py#L1559=), and the content is not extracted as expected.

A solution could be to implement a handler for `RadioEpisode` in `traverse_json_ld`. However, I don't think it makes sense to have extractor specific code (if it is?) in `common.py`, not to mention that's most likely way too deep in the internals of `yt-dlp` for my current knowledge. Moreover, it's also beyond the scope of this PR.

**Questions**:

- Is my understanding above correct?
- Should two other PRs come before that extractor can leverage `_search_json_ld`, one for the `break` → `continue` (at the risk of breaking other existing extractors), and another one to add a `RadioEpisode` handler that seems to be specific of this FranceCulture extractor so far, or
- Should the current PR be merged as is, or modified?

Parse URL query using regex is clumsy and less robust. To capture both aid and bvid, it would be like
```
    _VALID_URL = r'https?://player\.bilibili\.com/player\.html\?(?:.*?\baid=(?P<id>\d+))?(?:.*?\bbvid=(?P<id_bv>[^/?#&=]+))?(?:.*?\bpage=(?P<page>\d+))?'
```
Even that, the order of aid, bvid and page need to be fixed. 

Using standard url parser is much clean.

body:
comments returned by infodict is supposed to have a specific form
```
    comments:       A list of comments, each with one or more of the following
                    properties (all but one of text or html optional):
                        * "author" - human-readable name of the comment author
                        * "author_id" - user ID of the comment author
                        * "author_thumbnail" - The thumbnail of the comment author
                        * "id" - Comment ID
                        * "html" - Comment as HTML
                        * "text" - Plain text of the comment
                        * "timestamp" - UNIX timestamp of comment
                        * "parent" - ID of the comment this one is replying to.
                                     Set to "root" to indicate that this is a
                                     comment to the original video.
                        * "like_count" - Number of positive ratings of the comment
                        * "dislike_count" - Number of negative ratings of the comment
                        * "is_favorited" - Whether the comment is marked as
                                           favorite by the video uploader
                        * "author_is_uploader" - Whether the comment is made by
                                                 the video uploader
```

author: Grub4K

body:
`any` does the same thing as `get_all=False` but inline, so you can also use it for dict traversal:
```py
traverse_obj({0: [0, 1], 1: [None 1]}, {
    'a': [0, ..., any],
    'b': [1, ..., all],
})
```

See also the docstring, but the explanation is kinda bad. It sets branching to false and just takes the first item. Similarly `all` which sets branching to false and takes all items into a list.

PS: ideas for better developer documentation are welcome
author: Grub4K

body: Thank you! And I didn't realize the docstring has been updated. my bad. Let me commit the subsequent patch.
author: pzhlkj6612

body:
The example should be this:

```python
# -----------------------------------:
#                                    |
#                                    v
>>> traverse_obj({0: [0, 1], 1: [None, 1]}, {
        'a': [0, ..., any],
        'b': [1, ..., all],
    })

{'a': 0, 'b': [1]}

```

?

---

> ... It sets branching to false...

Hmmmm, the `traverse_obj` function becomes a bit fuzzier to me. 😄

> PS: ideas for better developer documentation are welcome

Okay. I've recorded it in my GitHub's [project board](https://github.com/pzhlkj6612?tab=projects).

imo. we should use just the new api instead of constructing the m3u8
```suggestion

        formats = [{'url': f'https://{direct_url}', 'format_id': 'http'}]
        subtitles = {}
        video_data = self._download_json(f'https://{direct_url}.json', video_id, fatal=False)
        if video_data:
            if hls_url := video_data.get('hls'):
                fmts, subs = self._extract_m3u8_formats_and_subtitles(
                    hls_url, video_id, m3u8_id='hls', fatal=False)
                formats.extend(fmts)
                self._merge_subtitles(subs, target=subtitles)
            if dash_url := video_data.get('mpd'):
                fmts, subs = self._extract_mpd_formats_and_subtitles(
                    dash_url, video_id, mpd_id='dash', fatal=False)
                formats.extend(fmts)
                self._merge_subtitles(subs, target=subtitles)

```

```suggestion
        if hls_url := traverse_obj(video_data, ('hls', {url_or_none})):
            fmts, subs = self._extract_m3u8_formats_and_subtitles(
                hls_url, video_id, m3u8_id='hls', fatal=False)
            formats.extend(fmts)
            self._merge_subtitles(subs, target=subtitles)
        if dash_url := traverse_obj(video_data, ('mpd', {url_or_none})):
            fmts, subs = self._extract_mpd_formats_and_subtitles(
                dash_url, video_id, mpd_id='dash', fatal=False)
            formats.extend(fmts)
            self._merge_subtitles(subs, target=subtitles)
```

body:
`.pub.name` identifies the newsletter, but `.pub.author_id` identifies the author. If you want to have ‘uploader’ mean ‘newsletter’ for this extractor, you should use `.pub.id` or `.post.publication_id` as `uploader_id`.

Also take note that some newsletters may have multiple authors. <https://www.mollymovieclub.com/p/interstellar> names two authors, but this extracts only one of them.

I remember there being some (proposal to add?) ‘crew’ credits metadata fields, maybe post authors (`.publishedBylines`) could be squeezed there.

author: pukkandan

body:
I don't think this behaves correctly. Pls double check

What we want (when regex fails):
* `fatal=True` => throw regex error
* `fatal=False` => print regex warning, but not json warning and return `{}`
* `default=xxx` => print neither warning and return `xxx` (not `json.loads(xxx)`)

(when json.loads fails):
* `fatal=True` => throw json error
* `fatal=False` => print json warning, and return `{}`
* `default=xxx` => print neither warning and return `xxx` (not `json.loads(xxx)`)

Note that `fatal` should be ignored when `default` is given

(split into 2 statements if needed)

You can merge once you confirm this behavior

>                    'decrypt_info': fragment_template['decrypt_info'],
>                    'byte_range': fragment_template['byte_range'],
>                    'media_sequence': fragment_template['media_sequence']}
> ```

This will yield the fragments in reverse, which is not what we want
author: pukkandan

body:
After looking over it I feel like the current implementation of _wait_for_video is not designed with playlists in mind. It will raise ReExtractInfo exception for reruning the extractor, and by that force many unnecessary website downloads to gain information that was already available from the start.

Perhaps to fix that _wait_for_video has to make reextraction optional (true by default) and otherwise run a method supplied in info_dict that will return the formats/url.

body:
> If it's difficult, don't worry about it

If it's difficult then I have an idea (it's quite hacky, I know):
If Python 3.10.7* exists, abort the workflow (so that people notice it)
If python 3.10.6 exists, use that and return a warning
Otherwise, use Python 3.10.5

*depending on how complicated you want to make the workflow, you could make that number higher, and do something like:
If Python 3.10.8 exists, abort the workflow (so that people notice it)
If python 3.10.7 or 3.10.6 exists, use that and return a warning
Otherwise, use Python 3.10.5

```suggestion
    def _download_webpage_handle(self, url_or_request, video_id, *args, fatal=True, **kwargs):
        response = super()._download_webpage_handle(url_or_request, video_id, *args, fatal=fatal, **kwargs)
        challenge_url, cookie = response[1].geturl() if response else '', None
        if challenge_url.startswith('https://vk.com/429.html?'):
            cookie = self._get_cookies(challenge_url).get('hash429')
        if not cookie:
            return response
            
        hash429 = hashlib.md5(cookie.value.encode('ascii')).hexdigest()
        self._request_webpage(
            update_url_query(challenge_url, {'key': hash429}), video_id, fatal=fatal,
            note='Resolving WAF challenge', errnote='Failed to bypass WAF challenge')
        return super()._download_webpage_handle(url_or_request, video_id, *args, fatal=True, **kwargs)
```

body:
There’s not much in there:

```python
{
  'title': 'WATCH: Welcoming Jess Bush to The Ready Room',
  'description': 'A closer look at the latest episode of Strange New Worlds',
  'thumbnails': [{'url': 'https://intl.startrek.com/sites/default/files/styles/video_1280x720/public/images/2022-06/pp_14794_rr_thumb_107_yt_16x9.jpg?h=d1cb525d&itok=pk5UgKFK'}],
  'timestamp': 1655388000,
  'uploader': 'StarTrek.com Staff'
}
```

* `title` is already extracted from the player’s `data-title` (but will be used as a fallback).
* `description` is different from the HTML description below the player and less detailed.
* `thumbnails` are extracted from `data-poster-url`. For the sake of simplicity I was leaning to not using the JSON-LD ones as a fallback either (because there can be multiple, while `data-poster-url` only has one). Also, I expect `data-poster-url` to be of the same or higher quality than the JSON-LD ones, because `data-poster-url` is what’s being used in the (large) player, while the JSON-LD one(s) are more likely to be smaller ones just for SEO & social media. If you disagree, let me know.
* `timestamp` is used.
* `uploader` does not look meaningful and is not shown to the user. Let me know if I should add it nevertheless.
author: scy

body:
> pukkandan seemed bust and since u r the other active maintainer, i added u aswell :)

Okay, why not

> I do not have any urls to test if removing int and the binary OR will result in any errors or not, are u sure there are no edge cases and the two formulas are extactly same?

In JavaScript, `value | 0` casts `value` into an integer (In python `12.6|0` throws error!)
Since the result of division is passed to `| 0`, `(vid / 5e3) | 0` is semantically equal to `vid // 5e3`

body:
I found the fps which bilibili returns are not several fixed floats,
so I mapped them to "nearest" standard fps, which I think contains [8, 16, 24, 25, 30, 48, 50, 60]
the "nearest" is defined by relative error, which is `abs(1 - fps / std_fps)`
the `max(3.0 / 60, 2.0 / 24)` is empirical parameters, I tested some case, it seems works well.

And I planed to review whether it can be simplied when I create the part2, but it passed many days since step1 PR, I almost forget it.


It is better to use minBy replace "sort and only take the first".

body:
1. This doesnt handle URLs like `...?foo=bar&ac=1234`
2. Unncessary groups
3. You are matching everything into id but the parsing it again later

```suggestion
    _VALID_URL = r'https?://www\.acfun\.cn/bangumi/(?P<id>aa[_\d]+)/?(?:\?([^#]+&)?ac=(?<ac_id>\d+))?'
```
Then in real_extract, you can just do
```py
video_id, ac_idx = self._match_valid_url('id', 'ac_id')
```

Alternatively, you can do only
```suggestion
    _VALID_URL = r'https?://www\.acfun\.cn/bangumi/(?P<id>aa[_\d]+)'
```
and in real_extract
```py
video_id = self._match_id(url)
ac_idx = parse_qs(url).get('ac', [None])[-1]
```

body:
why so complicated? Untested, but it looks like this is what u r trying to do:
```py
    def _get_ckey(self, video_id, url, app_version, platform):
        ua = self.get_param('http_headers')['User-Agent']
        
        payload = (f'{video_id}|{int(time.time())}|mg3c3b04ba|{app_version}|0000000000000000|'
                   f'{platform}|{url[:48]}|{ua.lower()[:48]}||Mozilla|Netscape|Win32|00|')

        ciphertext_bytes = aes_cbc_encrypt(
            f'|{sum(map(ord, payload))}|{payload}',
            b'Ok\xda\xa3\x9e/\x8c\xb0\x7f^r-\x9e\xde\xf3\x14',
            b'\x01PJ\xf3V\xe6\x19\xcf.B\xbb\xa6\x8c?p\xf9',
            'whitespace')

        return ''.join(f'{x:02x}' for x in ciphertext_bytes)
```

This function extract all video as single video. I mean the same video with different resolution treated as different video in `_extract_m3u8_formats_and_subtitles`. I don't know why this happen. 

The api is kind of weird too. The api return something with suffix `mp4` (eg: `https://video.internetvideoarchive.net/video.mp4?customerid=112548&cmd=6&e=253370764800&fmt=11&publishedid=579373&h=d239a70898d675940068c66aaf3340aa`) but return `m3u8`. body: The urls are same but id/name is different b/w uploader and channel?
author: pukkandan

body:
They are different things -  it is a bit confusing since patreon kinda mixes them together. For reference, my mapping is that channel is a campaign and uploader is a user.

The user url is coming directly from the API from attributes of a user type. For general users, this url is usually of the form `https://www.patreon.com/user?u=<user id>`. But it seems when a user has a campaign, such url seems to be of that of the campaign url.

Interesting observation: you can still technically access a channel that has a campaign through the user url (by manually forming it), and it will sometimes redirect to the campaign url. Other times it'll just load the campaign page.  E.g. https://www.patreon.com/user/posts?u=47770711. Might just be a bug.

So tl;dr this is expected

```suggestion
            err_json = self._parse_json(self._webpage_read_content(e.cause, None, item_id), item_id, fatal=False)
            err_message = traverse_obj(err_json, ('errors', ..., 'detail'), get_all=False)
            if err_message:
                raise ExtractorError(f'Patreon said: {err_message}', expected=True)
            raise
```

Though it's really only a personal preference, not a needed change

body:
Should url_or_none only be used when we are unsure if something will be returning a url? The developer instructions mention it is used for safe processing for URLs, but as far as I can tell it will always return a list of URLs from the webpages that I've viewed. If this can assumed, then the if condition is unneeded and I'll remove it.

Since we are on the topic of how to process the thumbnail image, I would like to mention that there is an og:image property but because of how merge_dicts work, the url in the json_ld which keeps the transformations would still be used if I don't pop the thumbnails element from the json_ld. Should I be popping the thumbnails property and use the regex on the og:image property or continue as is since https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/pull/4410#discussion_r928233254  shows concern of cherrypicking from json_ld.
author: Juhmer-Tena

body:
> Should url_or_none only be used when we are unsure if something will be returning a url? The developer instructions mention it is used for safe processing for URLs, but as far as I can tell it will always return a list of URLs from the webpages that I've viewed. If this can assumed, then the if condition is unneeded and I'll remove it.

The json_ld parser is already calling url_or_none https://github.com/yt-dlp/yt-dlp/blob/81bf0943eaa04069125dc683c418b65c2dbb7e25/yt_dlp/extractor/common.py#L1508-L1510

> but because of how merge_dicts work, the url in the json_ld which keeps the transformations would still be used if I don't pop the thumbnails element from the json_ld. 

You can reverse the order of the dicts in `merge_dicts` to prioritize the transformed info over json_ld

> Since we are on the topic of how to process the thumbnail image, I would like to mention that there is an og:image property ... Should I be popping the thumbnails property and use the regex on the og:image property

Is the og:image the same as the thumbnail json_ld extracts? Or is it a different thumbnail?
author: pukkandan

body: Thanks for the information! og:image is indeed the same thumbnail that the json_ld extracts, but with different transformations.

Isn't this better?
author: pukkandan

body:
I don't believe so. My interpretation of that regex is that it would just match the id minus one character if there's a playlist.
For example if we had the url `https://holodex.net/watch/1234?playlist=56`  it would match what it can for \w+ and return `https://holodex.net/watch/123` because 123 are all \w and the match isn't immediately followed by ?playlist= because it's followed by 4?playlist=
We could steal the url regex from the youtube IE and go with `[0-9A-Za-z_-]{11}(?!\?playlist=)` to make yours work but matching right after the id would also make it fail if playlist isn't the first parameter:
 `https://holodex.net/watch/1234?param=val&playlist=56`
It's a little inefficient scanning through the url twice but all the other solutions seem too brittle to me.
author: sqrtNOT

body:
> but matching right after the id would also make it fail if playlist isn't the first parameter:

your playlistIE regex has this same issue

body: ```suggestion 'title': 'Tagesschau + Sport am Sonntag - 31-07-2022 20:00', 'series': 'Tagesschau + Sport am Sonntag', 'upload_date': '20220731', 'thumbnail': r're:https://raisudtirol\.rai\.it/img/.+?\.jpg', 'uploader': 'raisudtirol', } }] def _real_extract(self, url): video_id = self._match_id(url) webpage = self._download_webpage(url, video_id) video_date = self._html_search_regex(r'(.+?)', webpage, 'video_date', fatal=False) video_title = self._html_search_regex(r'(.+?)', webpage, 'video_title', fatal=False) video_url = self._html_search_regex(r'sources:\s*\[\{file:\s*"(.+?)"\}\]', webpage, 'video_url') video_thumb = self._html_search_regex(r'image: \'(.+?)\'', webpage, 'video_thumb', fatal=False) return { 'id': video_id, 'title': join_nonempty(video_title, video_date, delim=' - '), 'series': video_title, 'upload_date': unified_strdate(video_date), 'thumbnail': urljoin('https://raisudtirol.rai.it/', video_thumb), ```

author: pukkandan

body:
Rationale: if there is a `v` that isn't a `dict`, it would definitely be a bad idea to crash extracting from it, whereas if no `v` at all gets extracted the extractor will hit `_sort_formats()` and break there.

https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/commit/5cb4833f408745135d1b0e178b9a2545a899f2ac

Possibly instead of checking for known media format IDs (`sd`, etc), you could have a check for `is_av_format()` in this loop and one for `is_sttl_format()` in the second loop that extracts subtitles.
author: dirkf

author: pukkandan

author: dirkf

body: BTW, there's another section below also listing `'sd', 'hd'` (where `urplayer_data['streamingInfo'].get('sweComplete')` is evaluated). I guess this tries to parse the subtitles' languages, and therefore probably doesn't apply to audio-only formats - but who prevents distribution of subtitles for audio anyway. ;-)
author: barsnick

body:
> Possibly instead of checking for known media format IDs (`sd`, etc), you could have a check for `is_av_format()` in this loop and one for `is_sttl_format()` in the second loop that extracts subtitles.

I don't understand how to implement `is_av_format()` and `is_sttl_format()` - as far as I can tell, the formats are recognized somewhere along the way by the framework - but I can see that both the `'raw'` and the `'sweComplete'` keys contain A/V media formats. And there's some code duplication by doing two iterations (one for A/V, one for subs).

Here's a suggestion on how they could be combined as such:
https://github.com/barsnick/yt-dlp/commits/urplay.08
Relevant code:
https://github.com/barsnick/yt-dlp/blob/790ec83b23b6e8b27e0202e0f1d5f8a4612bb3f3/yt_dlp/extractor/urplay.py#L106

Only caveat: It's still deciding on A/V vs. subs via the format name.

(`'sweComplete'` actually has a language key for the A/V formats, that's why I'm parsing it early, but I couldn't figure out how to attach the language property to the format in `formats.extend(self._extract_wowza_formats())`.)
author: barsnick

body:
> ('sweComplete' actually has a language key for the A/V formats, that's why I'm parsing it early, but I couldn't figure out how to attach the language property to the format in formats.extend(self._extract_wowza_formats()).)

```py
fmts = self._extract_wowza_formats(...)
for f in fmts:
    f['language'] = ...
formats.extend(fmts)
```

body:
Unsetting `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` on runtime would make it load undesired shared objects that PyInstaller didn't expect (i.e. load ones on system but bundled one by PI)

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19337926/why-change-in-ld-library-path-at-runtime-dosent-reflect-on-the-executable-once
> Changing `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` has no effect, except if the program calls after `dlopen` to load a plugin.

body: What if sessionid used expires? So the indicator used here is the info api, if it gets resolved then the user is logged in and if it doesn't then the user is not logged in. But the workflow is as mentioned by you, with an exception `/embed/captioned` not making into the code.
author: pritam20ps05

body: if the user is logged in we want them to bypass the `get_ruling_for_content` API step, and the `sessionid` cookie is the best indicator we have of whether the user is authenticated. if the cookie is expired, then the `media` API call will fail and the extractor can fall back on the logged-out flow (graphql -> webpage)
author: bashonly

body: Isn't the current code doing the same thing without the `sessionid` check?
author: pritam20ps05

body: But for cosmetic purposes i can add it anyways. The diff. is that just the failed media API call won't be made for non logged in users.
author: pritam20ps05

body:
> Isn't the current code doing the same thing without the `sessionid` check?

logged-out users are sending an unnecessary and counter-productive request to the mobile API (`i.instagram.com//media/{media_pk}/info/`) before the `api_check` & `graphql` request. this is working against their rate-limit. the `sessionid` check should happen before the mobile API request, and if it fails, the mobile API request should be bypassed (it is only useful for logged-in users)

body:
`;` is a valid character in a `quoted-string` cookie value.
```
>>> http.cookies.SimpleCookie('''chips="a;hoy"; vienna=finger''')
<SimpleCookie: chips='a;hoy' vienna='finger'>
>>> 
```
Check https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.10/Lib/http/cookies.py for what is currently being done (and has been for 22 years, according to the header comments).

What could additionally be done is to add a parallel pattern to the value match in `_CookiePattern`, s/t like this
```
r"""
(?:
  (?P<val>                         # Start of group 'val'
      "(?:[^\\"]|\\.)*"                  # Any doublequoted string
      |                                  # or
      \w{3},\s[\w\d\s-]{9,11}\s[\d:]{8}\sGMT  # Special case for "expires" attr
      |                                  # or
      [""" + _LegalValueChars + r"""]*      # Any word or empty string
      )|                                # End of group 'val'
  (?P<bad>(?:\\;|[^;])*)           # fallback match to skip bad value 
)
"""
```
Then in a rewrite of the `BaseCookie.__parse_string()` method, add a step to check the `bad` group: if it matches, skip past the match and try to parse the cookie string from there.
author: dirkf

body: Yeah cookie parsing is fairly complicated and needs to obey the relevant RFCs, so it's not going to be simple.
author: coletdjnz

body: OP's solution does look to be similar to what django has done to work around the issue. So it might be fine. https://github.com/django/django/commit/93a135d111c2569d88d65a3f4ad9e6d9ad291452, https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/26158
author: coletdjnz

body: RFC2109 has been obsoleted by RFC6265 and suggests semicolons are in fact not valid as can be read in [RFC6265, Section 4.1.1](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6265#section-4.1.1) about `cookie-octet` and `cookie-name` (which forwards to [RFC2616, Section 2.2, `token`](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2616#section-2.2)), which my code should be compliant with. Interpreting the key and value to quote characters might be problematic.
author: Grub4K

body: Even so, follow CPython as closely as possible, making only minimum required change to fix our issue
author: pukkandan

body: In my opinion the current way of parsing the cookie string cannot easily be fixed to handle the strings as required.
author: Grub4K

body: I have implemented a wrapper function `__make_simple_cookie()` which closely resembles the parsing done in `http.cookies` (except for the `bad` group and not failing on errors). Please review anew.
author: Grub4K

```suggestion
        index_page = self._download_webpage(f'https://www.redgifs.com/watch/{video_id}', video_id)
        index_js_uri = self._html_search_regex(
            r'href="?(/assets/js/index[.a-z0-9]*.js)"?\W',index_page, 'index_js_uri')
        index_js = self._download_webpage(f'https://www.redgifs.com/{index_js_uri}', video_id)

        # It turns out that a { followed by any valid JSON punctuation will always result in the
        # first two characters of the base64 encoding being "ey".
        # Use this fact to find any such string constant of a reasonable length with the correct
        # punctuation for an oauth token
        oauth_token = self._html_search_regex(
            r'\w+\s*[=:]\s*"(ey[^"]+\.[^"]*\.[^"]{43,45})"', index_js, 'oauth token')
        self._API_HEADERS['authorization'] = f'Bearer {oauth_token}'
```

body: From here : https://github.com/ytdl-org/youtube-dl/pull/13194/files#diff-301769c0cffc70e23f61421e11e68f1c77bf60db2f7547430bd3c4c894609699R40
author: LucBerge

body:
Leaving the key in the repository may not be the best idea. Deezer has sent [DMCAs](https://github.com/github/dmca/blob/master/2021/02/2021-02-10-deezer.md) to many repos containing it in the past. It specifically mentions `"To do so, these repositories are sharing the private encryption keys retrieved maliciously to bypass Deezer's security measures to unlawfully download its music catalogue, directly from Deezer’s servers in total violation of our rights and those of our music licensors (phonographic producers, performing artists, songwriters and composers)."`

It might be a better idea to [dynamically generate the key](https://github.com/yne/dzr/issues/11) or just make users provide it themselves.
author: Ryan5453

body: @ryan5453 I agree, this is a good idea. Note that this key is not directly used to decrypt the song. It is used to compute a new key that will decrypt the song.

traversing is not an expensive operation. This results in much simpler/cleaner code
```py
        season = traverse_obj(data_json, ('container', 'title'))
        return {
            ...,
            'series': traverse_obj(data_json, ('container', 'container', 'title')),
            'season': season,
            'episode_number': data_json.get('sequence'),
            'season_number': int_or_none(self._search_regex(r'Season (\d+)', season, 'Season Number', default=None)),
        }
```

The md5 checksum is computed by the test runner, taking the field as input (in this case "description"). As pukkandan said, in this case is okay because it's short but if it were a description with >150 characters we would prefer md5

body:
> Do we have to retry on 404?

wayback machine is a bit finicky and will sometimes return 404 even if it is archived, hence the original intent for retry. 

but someone suggested in the discord that they will cache it so retrying on this may not help anyways.

should keep it for other network errors, though.

Py2exe increases that number every time there's a breaking change. I imagine that's why @SG5 decided to pin it.

That said, it is a bit of a double edged sword, because I highly doubt anyone here will notice when Py2exe `0.13` gets released, and then yt-dlp will be using an outdated version of py2exe

author: dirkf

body:
In the original extractor the logic went like this:

* look for anything in `status['extended_entities']['media'][0]`, extract from that
* otherwise look for status['card'], extract whatever from there
* otherwise try status['entities']['urls'][0]['expanded_url']

The final case wouldn't have run for any truthy value of `status.get('card')`.
author: dirkf

body: In case it wasn't obvious, I set the `id` for each of multiple videos from the `id_str` in the API response (and the `title` had `#{n}` appended) to make them distinct, but the original `title` and `id` were restored if only one video was found, giving the original result. In the multiple case, the playlist retained the original `id` and would be the `playlist_id` in the output JSON.
author: dirkf

body:
What is your reasoning behind restoring the old `id`? I find it beneficial to provide the actual `media_id` instead, since thats consistent with the behavior of the playlist variation.

Changing the title to reflect media index makes a lot of sense. I will implement that as well.
author: Grub4K

body:
> What is your reasoning behind restoring the old id?

Likely because youtube-dl does not have `_old_archive_ids`
author: pukkandan

body:
I would like some (external) feedback on this. Do we need to mirror old behavior?

Since we extract multiple elements anyways, why not also use `card` extraction whenever possible?

From what I can see in the [docs](https://developer.twitter.com/en/docs/twitter-api/v1/data-dictionary/object-model/entities#urls) that final case only makes sense if we want to follow redirects on no found media. Personally I would not want such behavior at all.

In the case of scrapping it we could just return an empty playlist (remove the `if`) or `ExtractorError` immediately.
author: Grub4K

body:
Just that I wanted to return the same `id` as would have been returned by the single video extractor when the API response had a video in `status['extended_entities']['media'][0]`.

Setting the `display_id` to the tweet id and using the video's `id_str` as `id` would be reasonable but not backward compatible. But I suppose that's what `_old_archive_ids` addresses?

I also trimmed the title to 120 characters, but that's another issue.

It sounds to me altchars only replaces + and / instead of an arbitrary set of characters.
